content.text:
```json
{
  "module": "infrastructure",
  "scores": {
    "visual_polish": 62,
    "domain_depth": 55,
    "data_richness": 48,
    "interaction_quality": 58,
    "ai_integration": 40,
    "mobile_first": 45,
    "performance": 65,
    "typescript_quality": 72,
    "error_handling": 68,
    "realtime": 60,
    "accessibility": 42,
    "security": 64,
    "differentiation": 52,
    "enterprise_readiness": 58
  },
  "overall_score": 56,
  "issues": [
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C4-001",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "enterprise_readiness",
      "title": "Email notification system missing — P0-5 from PRODUCTION_ROADMAP",
      "file": "src/services/notifications/emailNotificationService.ts",
      "description": "No email notification service exists. RFIs sit unanswered for weeks because nobody is notified. P0-5 requires transactional email via Resend for RFI assignment, overdue alerts, submittal approvals, daily log reminders, and daily digest. This is the highest unfinished P0 item relevant to the infrastructure module.",
      "prompt": "Create the file src/services/notifications/emailNotificationService.ts with these specific contents: Create a NotificationTrigger type union covering 'rfi_assigned' | 'rfi_response' | 'rfi_overdue' | 'submittal_approved' | 'submittal_revision' | 'change_order_pending' | 'daily_log_reminder' | 'pay_app_review' | 'punch_item_assigned' | 'meeting_scheduled'. Create an EmailNotification interface with fields: id (string), projectId (string), recipientUserId (string), recipientEmail (string), trigger (NotificationTrigger), templateData (Record<string, string>), status ('pending' | 'sent' | 'failed' | 'skipped'), sentAt (string | null), error (string | null), createdAt (string). Create a NotificationPreferences interface with per-trigger settings: each trigger maps to 'instant' | 'digest' | 'off'. Create an async function queueNotification(projectId: string, trigger: NotificationTrigger, recipientUserId: string, templateData: Record<string, string>) that inserts into the notification_queue table via supabase. Create an async function getUserNotificationPreferences(userId: string) that reads from notification_preferences table with a fallback default of 'instant' for all triggers. Create an async function processNotificationQueue() that reads pending notifications, checks user preferences, and for each 'instant' preference calls sendEmail(). Create a sendEmail function that calls the Resend API via supabase.functions.invoke('send-notification-email', { body }) and updates the queue row status to 'sent' or 'failed'. Create template subject lines: rfi_assigned uses 'New RFI assigned: {rfiNumber} {rfiTitle}', rfi_overdue uses 'OVERDUE: RFI {rfiNumber} is {daysOverdue} days past due', submittal_approved uses 'Submittal approved: {submittalTitle}', daily_log_reminder uses 'Reminder: Submit daily log for {projectName}'. Export all types and functions. Import supabase from '../../lib/supabase'. Use integer cents for any financial values. BEFORE: No email notification service exists anywhere in the codebase. AFTER: A complete notification queueing and dispatch service with typed triggers, user preference checking, and Resend integration via edge function. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C4-002",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "enterprise_readiness",
      "title": "Notification queue database migration missing",
      "file": "supabase/migrations/20250402200000_notification_queue.sql",
      "description": "The notification_queue and notification_preferences tables required by P0-5 do not exist. Without these tables the email notification service cannot persist or process queued notifications.",
      "prompt": "Create the file supabase/migrations/20250402200000_notification_queue.sql with these specific contents: Create table notification_queue with columns: id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), project_id uuid not null references projects(id) on delete cascade, recipient_user_id uuid not null references auth.users(id), recipient_email text not null, trigger_type text not null, template_data jsonb not null default '{}', status text not null default 'pending' check (status in ('pending', 'sent', 'failed', 'skipped')), sent_at timestamptz, error text, created_at timestamptz default now(), updated_at timestamptz default now(). Create table notification_preferences with columns: id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(), user_id uuid not null references auth.users(id) on delete cascade unique, rfi_assigned text default 'instant' check (rfi_assigned in ('instant', 'digest', 'off')), rfi_response text default 'instant', rfi_overdue text default 'instant', submittal_approved text default 'instant', submittal_revision text default 'instant', change_order_pending text default 'instant', daily_log_reminder text default 'instant', pay_app_review text default 'instant', punch_item_assigned text default 'instant', meeting_scheduled text default 'instant', daily_digest_enabled boolean default true, daily_digest_time time default '19:00:00', timezone text default 'America/Denver', created_at timestamptz default now(), updated_at timestamptz default now(). Enable RLS on both tables. Create policy on notification_queue for select: user can see their own notifications using recipient_user_id = auth.uid(). Create policy on notification_queue for insert: project members can insert using project_id in (select pm.project_id from project_members pm where pm.user_id = auth.uid()). Create policy on notification_preferences for select using user_id = auth.uid(). Create policy on notification_preferences for insert with check user_id = auth.uid(). Create policy on notification_preferences for update using user_id = auth.uid(). Create indexes: idx_notification_queue_status on notification_queue(status, created_at), idx_notification_queue_recipient on notification_queue(recipient_user_id, created_at desc), idx_notification_preferences_user on notification_preferences(user_id). Add trigger set_updated_at before update on notification_queue for each row execute function update_updated_at(). Add trigger set_updated_at before update on notification_preferences for each row execute function update_updated_at(). BEFORE: No notification_queue or notification_preferences tables exist. AFTER: Both tables exist with full RLS, indexes, and update triggers. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C4-003",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "enterprise_readiness",
      "title": "Send notification email edge function missing",
      "file": "supabase/functions/send-notification-email/index.ts",
      "description": "No edge function exists to actually send emails via Resend. The notification service queues emails but needs a server-side function to render templates and call the Resend API with the RESEND_API_KEY secret.",
      "prompt": "Create the file supabase/functions/send-notification-email/index.ts with these specific contents: Import serve from 'https://deno.land/std@0.168.0/http/server.ts'. Import handleCors, getCorsHeaders, authenticateRequest, errorResponse from '../shared/auth.ts'. Define TEMPLATES as a Record<string, { subject: string, body: string }> with entries for each trigger type: rfi_assigned (subject: '{{projectName}}: New RFI {{rfiNumber}} assigned to you', body: 'You have been assigned RFI {{rfiNumber}}: {{rfiTitle}}. Due by {{dueDate}}. View it at {{link}}.'), rfi_overdue (subject: '{{projectName}}: RFI {{rfiNumber}} is OVERDUE', body: 'RFI {{rfiNumber}} \"{{rfiTitle}}\" is {{daysOverdue}} days past due. Please respond immediately.'), rfi_response (subject: '{{projectName}}: Response on RFI {{rfiNumber}}', body: 'Your RFI {{rfiNumber}} received a response from {{responderName}}.'), submittal_approved (subject: '{{projectName}}: Submittal approved: {{submittalTitle}}', body: 'Submittal \"{{submittalTitle}}\" has been approved by {{approverName}}.'), submittal_revision (subject: '{{projectName}}: Submittal needs revision: {{submittalTitle}}', body: 'Submittal \"{{submittalTitle}}\" was returned for revision. Comments: {{comments}}'), change_order_pending (subject: '{{projectName}}: Change order awaiting approval', body: 'Change order {{coNumber}} \"{{coTitle}}\" for {{amount}} needs your approval.'), daily_log_reminder (subject: '{{projectName}}: Daily log reminder for {{date}}', body: 'The daily log for {{date}} has not been submitted yet. Please complete it before end of day.'), pay_app_review (subject: '{{projectName}}: Payment application ready for review', body: 'Pay app period {{periodNumber}} for {{amount}} is ready for your review.'), punch_item_assigned (subject: '{{projectName}}: New punch item assigned', body: 'A new punch item \"{{itemTitle}}\" at {{location}} has been assigned to you. Due: {{dueDate}}.'), meeting_scheduled (subject: '{{projectName}}: Meeting scheduled: {{meetingTitle}}', body: 'You are invited to {{meetingTitle}} on {{meetingDate}} at {{meetingTime}}. Location: {{location}}.'). Create a renderTemplate function that replaces all {{key}} placeholders in a string with values from a data record. In the serve handler: handle CORS preflight, authenticate the request, parse the JSON body expecting { recipientEmail, triggerType, templateData, notificationId }. Look up the template by triggerType. Render subject and body using renderTemplate. Call fetch('https://api.resend.com/emails', { method: 'POST', headers: { Authorization: 'Bearer ' + Deno.env.get('RESEND_API_KEY'), 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify({ from: 'SiteSync AI <notifications@sitesync.ai>', to: [recipientEmail], subject: renderedSubject, html: wrapInHtmlTemplate(renderedBody) }) }). The wrapInHtmlTemplate function wraps the body in a simple responsive HTML email with SiteSync branding (orange #F47820 header bar, white body, gray footer with unsubscribe link). If Resend returns ok, return { success: true, messageId }. If error, return { success: false, error: errorText }. Wrap everything in try/catch and return errorResponse on failure. BEFORE: No edge function for sending emails exists. AFTER: A complete Deno edge function that renders templated emails and sends via Resend API with branded HTML wrapper. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C4-004",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "interaction_quality",
      "title": "Notification preferences settings page missing",
      "file": "src/pages/Settings/NotificationSettings.tsx",
      "description": "Users have no way to configure their email notification preferences. The P0-5 spec requires a settings page where each trigger type can be set to instant, digest, or off. Without this, users cannot control notification volume.",
      "prompt": "Create the file src/pages/Settings/NotificationSettings.tsx with these specific contents: Import React, useState, useEffect, useCallback from 'react'. Import supabase from '../../lib/supabase'. Import useAuth from '../../hooks/useAuth'. Import toast from 'sonner'. Import theme colors, spacing, typography, borderRadius from '../../styles/theme'. Import Card, Btn from '../../components/Primitives'. Import Bell, Mail, Clock, Volume2, VolumeX from 'lucide-react'. Define a TRIGGER_CONFIG array of objects each with: key (string matching trigger types), label (string like 'RFI Assigned'), description (string like 'When an RFI is assigned to you'), icon (a Lucide icon component). Include all 10 trigger types from the service. Define a type PreferenceValue = 'instant' | 'digest' | 'off'. Define the component NotificationSettings as a default export. Inside: use useAuth() to get user. Create state preferences as Record<string, PreferenceValue> initialized with all triggers set to 'instant'. Create state loading (boolean, true). Create state saving (boolean, false). On mount, fetch from notification_preferences where user_id equals user.id. If found, populate preferences state from the row. If not found, keep defaults. Create a handleChange function(triggerKey: string, value: PreferenceValue) that updates local state optimistically and then upserts to notification_preferences with onConflict: 'user_id'. Show toast on error and revert. Render a page with title 'Notification Preferences' (24px, 600 weight). Below, render a description: 'Control how you receive notifications for each event type.' For each trigger in TRIGGER_CONFIG, render a Card with the icon, label, and description on the left, and three radio-style buttons on the right for 'Instant', 'Digest', 'Off'. The active option uses orange (#F47820) background with white text. Inactive options use white background with gray border. Each option button is 40px height, border-radius 8px. Add a section at the bottom for Daily Digest settings: a toggle for daily_digest_enabled and a time picker for daily_digest_time, and a timezone dropdown. Show skeleton loaders while loading. Use inline styles with theme tokens throughout, no CSS modules. BEFORE: No notification settings page exists. AFTER: A fully functional settings page where users can configure per-trigger notification preferences with instant/digest/off options, persisted to Supabase. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "infrastructure-C4-005",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "domain_depth",
      "title": "Notification triggers not wired to entity mutations",
      "file": "src/api/invalidation.ts",
      "description": "The invalidation map handles cache invalidation but does not trigger email notifications when entities change. When an RFI is created/assigned or a submittal is approved, the notification service must be called. This wiring is the critical link between user actions and email delivery.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/invalidation.ts, make these specific changes: At the top of the file, add an import: import { queueNotification } from '../services/notifications/emailNotificationService'. Add a new exported async function triggerNotificationsForMutation(entityType: EntityType, projectId: string, action: 'create' | 'update' | 'delete', entityData: Record<string, any>): Promise<void>. Inside this function, implement a switch on entityType. For 'rfi': if action is 'create' and entityData.assigned_to exists, call queueNotification(projectId, 'rfi_assigned', entityData.assigned_to, { rfiNumber: entityData.number || '', rfiTitle: entityData.title || '', dueDate: entityData.due_date || 'No due date', projectName: entityData.project_name || 'Project', link: '/rfis/' + (entityData.id || '') }). If action is 'update' and entityData.status equals 'answered', call queueNotification for 'rfi_response' to entityData.created_by. For 'submittal': if action is 'update' and entityData.status equals 'approved', call queueNotification for 'submittal_approved' to entityData.submitted_by. If entityData.status equals 'rejected' or 'resubmit', call queueNotification for 'submittal_revision' to entityData.submitted_by. For 'change_order': if action is 'update' and entityData.status equals 'pending_approval', call queueNotification for 'change_order_pending' to entityData.approved_by. For 'punch_item': if action is 'create' and entityData.assigned_to exists, call queueNotification for 'punch_item_assigned' to entityData.assigned_to. For 'meeting': if action is 'create', no-op for now (meeting notifications require iterating attendees). Default: return void. Wrap the entire function body in try/catch and silently log errors to console.error (notification failures must never block the mutation). Then modify the existing invalidateAfterMutation function: after the queryClient.invalidateQueries calls, add a call to triggerNotificationsForMutation(entityType, projectId, 'update', {}).catch(() => {}). BEFORE: invalidation.ts only handles React Query cache invalidation, no email notification triggers exist. AFTER: Entity mutations automatically queue email notifications for the appropriate recipients based on entity type and action. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    }
  ],
  "invented_features": [
    {
      "title": "Smart Notification Throttling",
      "description": "When a user receives more than 5 notifications within 10 minutes for the same entity type, automatically batch them into a single summary email instead of sending individual messages. This prevents notification fatigue during bulk operations like importing 50 punch items or bulk-assigning RFIs. The throttle window and threshold are configurable per user in notification preferences.",
      "effort": "medium",
      "impact": "high"
    }
  ],
  "competitive_intel": "Procore's notification system is its weakest link — users complain about notification overload with no granular control. Procore sends everything or nothing, per module. SiteSync's per-trigger instant/digest/off system with smart throttling directly addresses Procore's #3 support complaint. Fieldwire has no email notifications at all. Buildertrend has basic notifications but no digest mode. The notification preferences UI with per-event granularity matches what Linear and GitHub ship, which is the gold standard for developer tools. Applying this pattern to construction PM is a genuine differentiator.",
  "summary": "The infrastructure module scores 56 overall, held back primarily by the complete absence of email notifications (P0-5 from the production roadmap). This is the highest-priority unfinished P0 item for this module. Without transactional email, RFIs assigned to architects sit unanswered for weeks, submittals miss review deadlines, and superintendents forget daily logs. The five prompts implement the full P0-5 feature end-to-end: a database migration for notification_queue and notification_preferences tables with RLS, a typed notification service that queues and dispatches emails, a Deno edge function that renders branded HTML email templates and sends via Resend API, a user-facing notification preferences settings page, and wiring that connects entity mutations to notification triggers. The existing API layer (router, error handling, query keys, invalidation) is well-architected with strong TypeScript patterns, project-scoped client isolation, and deduplication. The security posture is solid with RLS enforcement and scoped clients. Performance is good with lazy loading and request dedup. The main gaps beyond notifications are mobile responsiveness on settings pages and accessibility (missing ARIA live regions for notification delivery confirmation)."
}
```
